From Targeted to Universal Agents

Outline (From Targeted to Universal Agents)

  1. Introduction [2]
  2. From Targeted to Universal Agents [3]
  3. Agent Skills [3]
  4. Model Context Protocol (MCP) [3]
  5. Declarative Workflows [6]
  6. Wrap up [2]
From Targeted to Universal Agents Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(6) The Age of Universal Agents

  • Agents are evolving from single-purpose tools to broad-capability systems
  • Each new capability adds tools — and decisions — to the agent's scope
  • More capability → more decisions → more room for error
  • The question is not whether agents can do more. It's how to make them do it reliably.


From Targeted to Universal Agents Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(7) The Tool Problem

  • Tool overload: too many options degrade agent decision quality
  • Tool selection is itself a reasoning task — and it fails at scale
  • Three patterns address this directly: Agent Skills, MCP, and Arazzo Workflows
  • We will use a single scenario throughout: Your API has just landed in your repo. How do you ensure it is AI-ready?


From Targeted to Universal Agents Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(8) The Reproducibility Ladder

Four approaches to agent capability — increasing determinism at each rung:

Rung Execution LLM Involvement Reproducibility
Prompting LLM (unguided) Full Low
Agent Skills LLM (guided) Within constraints Medium
MCP Tools Tool engine Discovery + Invocation Medium-High
Arazzo Workflows Workflow engine Trigger only High

Scenario thread: “Your OpenAPI description just landed in your repo. You need it scored for AI-readiness and improved. Watch what happens at each rung.”

* LLMs can also author and register Arazzo workflows — converting observed processes into reusable, deterministic assets stored for future agent use.



Agent Skills

Outline (Agent Skills)

  1. Introduction [2]
  2. From Targeted to Universal Agents [3]
  3. Agent Skills [3]
  4. Model Context Protocol (MCP) [3]
  5. Declarative Workflows [6]
  6. Wrap up [2]
Agent Skills Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(10) What Is an Agent Skill?

  • Structured, reusable context for a specific subtask
  • Lives in the LLM's context window — shapes how it thinks and acts
  • Explicit scope: does one thing, with guardrails, in a defined way
  • Versioned artifact — reviewed, shared, and reused across agents

At this rung: We load the jentic-openapi-audit skill. The LLM knows exactly what tool to call, what format to produce, what not to do. Consistent output. Reusable across teams.



Agent Skills Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(11) Anatomy of a Skill

A SKILL.md defines: task · constraints · steps · references to external scripts or tools

  • Task: what the skill is for — clear, bounded scope
  • Constraints: what the LLM must and must not do
  • Steps: how to approach the task, in order
  • References: external scripts, tools, or MCP capabilities to invoke

Versioned, shareable, and loadable by any compatible agent. Think of it as a reusable prompt with structure and guardrails baked in.

A skill tells the LLM how to think about a task. A skill can discover and trigger a workflow — once it does, the LLM is out of the execution loop.



Agent Skills Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(12) Agent Skills: When to Use

  • Use when: recurring subtask needing LLM judgment · consistent output required · reusability matters
  • Not for: multi-step processes with a defined sequence · capabilities needed by multiple systems
  • Reproducibility: Medium — guided, not guaranteed
  • A skill can trigger a workflow. Once triggered, the LLM is out of the execution loop.


Model Context Protocol (MCP)

Outline (Model Context Protocol (MCP))

  1. Introduction [2]
  2. From Targeted to Universal Agents [3]
  3. Agent Skills [3]
  4. Model Context Protocol (MCP) [3]
  5. Declarative Workflows [6]
  6. Wrap up [2]
Model Context Protocol (MCP) Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(14) What Is MCP?

  • Standardised interface for exposing discrete tools to agents
  • Execution moves outside the LLM's reasoning space
  • LLM decides when to call; tool executes deterministically
  • Local or remote; versioned independently of the agent

At this rung: The scoring engine is an MCP tool. Any agent can call it. The LLM does not run the scoring logic — it just invokes the tool.



Model Context Protocol (MCP) Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(15) MCP: Tool Access and Organisation

  • Before: 50+ tools in the LLM's context window — open buffet, high cognitive load
  • After: MCP server as a capability router — structured access, reduced noise
  • Multiple agents, one server = reuse without coupling
  • Access control: who can call what, from where


Model Context Protocol (MCP) Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(16) MCP: When to Use

  • Use when: discrete callable capability · multiple callers · deterministic execution
  • Not for: multi-step process orchestration · LLM reasoning tasks
  • Reproducibility: Medium-High

The limitation: MCP works well for single-tool invocations. But each capability needs a server — built, deployed, and maintained. As known, repeatable processes grow, so does the catalog. You end up hardcoding sequences and maintaining them indefinitely.

What if you could describe the sequence declaratively instead — and let the execution engine handle it?



Declarative Workflows

Outline (Declarative Workflows)

  1. Introduction [2]
  2. From Targeted to Universal Agents [3]
  3. Agent Skills [3]
  4. Model Context Protocol (MCP) [3]
  5. Declarative Workflows [6]
  6. Wrap up [2]
Declarative Workflows Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(18) The Problem Workflows Solve

Problem 1 — Non-determinism:

  • Same prompt, three runs, three outcomes. Token waste. No audit trail.
  • At one agent, a bug. At a hundred, a governance crisis.

Problem 2 — MCP catalog sprawl:

  • As repeatable processes multiply, so does the server catalog
  • You have traded LLM unpredictability for infrastructure maintenance

Can you describe the process once, run it identically every time, and not maintain a server to do it?



Declarative Workflows Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(19) What Is Arazzo?

  • Declarative: define the sequence, data flow, and success criteria in a single document
  • The OpenAPI Initiative [https://www.openapis.org/] standard for multi-step API workflow descriptions
  • Arazzo workflows reference operationIds from OpenAPI descriptions (soon also AsyncAPI descriptions)
  • Discoverable by agents (via MCP or as APIs): find and trigger a workflow without knowing its internals 
workflows:
  - workflowId: scoreAndImproveAPI
    steps:
      - stepId: runScoring
        operationId: scoreAPIDescription
        successCriteria:
          - condition: $statusCode == 200
      - stepId: applyImprovements
        operationId: applyNonBreakingFixes
        inputs:
          issues: $steps.runScoring.outputs.issues


Declarative Workflows Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(20) The Full Scoring Workflow

An agent is asked: How AI-ready is this API? — whether by a developer, a CI/CD event, or a governance check:

  1. Agent discovers the scoreAndImproveAPI Arazzo workflow
  2. Agent triggers it once — the engine takes over
  3. Fetch spec → score for AI-readiness → apply non-breaking improvements
  4. Generate before/after report → return structured results to the agent or PR

Every run is identical. Every result is auditable. The LLM does not need to know the steps.

No MCP server to maintain. No prompt to tune. No guessing what the agent did.



Declarative Workflows Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(21) Workflows: When to Use

  • Use when: multi-step process · defined sequence · auditability required · enterprise or regulated context
  • Not for: exploratory tasks · tasks needing LLM judgment at each step
  • Reproducibility: High


Declarative Workflows Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(22) Should You Build an Agent? Now Answer It.

Based on Anthropic's Should I Build an Agent? framework — extended with the full toolkit:

Question Coarse answer With the full toolkit
Is the task complex enough? No → Workflow No → Arazzo. Yes but needs context → Skill first, leveraging MCP tools as needed; once the path is known, graduate to Arazzo.
Is the task valuable enough? <$0.10/run → Workflow · >$1 → Agent Low-cost repeatable → Arazzo. Needs LLM judgment each time → Skill.
Are all steps known and fixed? Yes → Workflow Yes → Arazzo. Context-dependent → Skill guides, Arazzo executes.
What is the cost of error? High → Workflow + human gate High → Arazzo with decision points. Shared capability → MCP.

The question was never just agent vs. workflow. It was: what level of determinism does this task actually need?



Declarative Workflows Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(23) How They Layer: The Full Picture

Prompting Agent Skills MCP Tools Arazzo Workflows
Execution LLM LLM (guided) Tool engine Workflow engine
LLM role Full Within constraints Discovery + Invocation Trigger only
Reproducibility Low Medium Medium-High High
Maintenance None Low Medium (servers) Low (declarative)
Best for Exploration Guided reasoning Shared capabilities Repeatable processes
Failure mode Non-determinism Drift Catalog sprawl Scope rigidity

In the scoring scenario: Skill gives context → MCP exposes the capability → Arazzo automates the pipeline. Each rung reduces maintenance burden while increasing determinism.

Key distinction: A skill tells the LLM how to think about a task (flexible path). A workflow tells the system what to do, step by step, without asking the LLM (fixed path). They compose: skills discover and trigger workflows.



Wrap up

Outline (Wrap up)

  1. Introduction [2]
  2. From Targeted to Universal Agents [3]
  3. Agent Skills [3]
  4. Model Context Protocol (MCP) [3]
  5. Declarative Workflows [6]
  6. Wrap up [2]
Wrap up Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(25) What to Do Next

  1. Score your current APIs — know how ready they are to enable agents before your agents find out the hard way
  2. Identify your capabilities — where are you on the ladder? What level of reproducibility does each task actually need?
  3. Ask: who else needs this capability? — if it's agents only, Arazzo may be enough; if humans, other systems, and agents all need it, you need more than just MCP
  4. Highest-risk recurring task → write a skill for it
  5. Capability needed by multiple systems → wrap it in MCP
  6. Multi-step business process → describe it in Arazzo
  • Arazzo Specification [https://spec.openapis.org/arazzo/latest.html]
  • Jentic API Scorecard [https://jentic.com/scorecard]
  • API AI-Readiness Framework [https://github.com/jentic/api-ai-readiness-framework]


Wrap up Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(26) Thank You! Let's Connect!
